questions.question:
Consider the relations r1(P, Q, R) and r2(R, S, T) with primary keys P and R respectively. The relation r1 contains 2000 tuples and r2 contains 2500 tuples. The maximum size of the join r1 |><| r2 is:
A. 2000
B. 30400
C. 38400
D. 798400
questions.to_find: Maximum number of tuples in the natural join r1 |><| r2

understand.plain: We have two relations joined on their common attribute R. Since R is the primary key of r2, each tuple in r1 can match at most one tuple in r2. Therefore the join size is bounded by the size of r1, which is 2000.

keywords.explain: An attribute (or set) that uniquely identifies each tuple in a relation; no two tuples share the same primary key value.
keywords.example: In r2(R, S, T), R is the PK, so every R value in r2 is unique.

keywords.explain: A join that matches tuples from two relations on all common attributes and removes duplicate columns.
keywords.example: r1 |><| r2 joins on attribute R, the common attribute.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of result tuples is limited by the cardinality of the smaller relation when the join attribute is a primary key in one relation.
keywords.example: If R is PK of r2, each r1 tuple matches at most one r2 tuple, so |r1 |><| r2| <= |r1|.

understand.visual_alt: r1 has 2000 tuples with PK P; r2 has 2500 tuples with PK R. The join is on attribute R. Because R is the primary key of r2, every r1 tuple can match at most one r2 tuple.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of tuples in the natural join r1 |><| r2.

terms.meaning: Relation r1 with attributes P, Q, R; P is the primary key.
terms.example: Tuple (p1, q1, r3) is one row in r1.
terms.connects: r1 has 2000 tuples and shares attribute R with r2.

terms.meaning: Relation r2 with attributes R, S, T; R is the primary key.
terms.example: Tuple (r3, s1, t2) is one row in r2.
terms.connects: Since R is PK of r2, each R value is unique in r2; so each r1 tuple matches at most one r2 tuple.

terms.example: r1 has 2000 rows and r2 has 2500 rows.
terms.connects: The join result cannot exceed min(2000, 2500) = 2000 because R is PK of r2.

given.plan: Because R is the primary key of r2, every value of R appears at most once in r2. Hence each tuple in r1 can join with at most one tuple in r2, giving a maximum join size of |r1| = 2000.

steps.title: Identify the join attribute and key constraint
steps.formula_raw: r1 |><| r2 joins on attribute R
steps.apply: The common attribute between r1(P,Q,R) and r2(R,S,T) is R. R is declared the primary key of r2, so every R value in r2 is distinct.
steps.note: The primary key constraint on R in r2 is the crucial fact that limits the join size.

steps.title: Apply the maximum join size bound
steps.formula_raw: max |r1 |><| r2| = min(|r1|, |r2|) when join attribute is PK in one relation
steps.apply: Since R is PK in r2, each r1 tuple matches at most 1 tuple in r2. So the join can have at most |r1| = 2000 tuples. (All 2000 r1 tuples could have their R value present in r2.)
steps.note: If R were not a key in r2, a single r1 tuple could match multiple r2 tuples, potentially giving up to |r1| * |r2| = 5,000,000 tuples. But the PK constraint caps it at 2000.

solution.result: Maximum size of r1 |><| r2 is 2000. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: When the join attribute is a primary key in one of the relations, each tuple in the other relation matches at most one tuple, bounding the join size by the cardinality of that other relation.

questions.question:
Consider the following relation schemas:
b-Schema = (b-name, b-city, assets)
a-Schema = (a-num, b-name, bal)
d-Schema = (c-name, a-number)

Let branch, account and depositor be respectively instances of the above schemas. Assume that account and depositor relations are much bigger than the branch relation.

Consider the following query:
Pi_{c-name}(sigma_{b-city = 'Agra'}(branch) |><| (bal < 0) account |><| depositor)

Which one of the following is the most efficient version of the above query?

A. Pi_{c-name}(sigma_{b-city = 'Agra'}(branch |><| account) |><| (bal < 0) depositor)
B. Pi_{c-name}(sigma_{b-city = 'Agra'}(branch) |><| sigma_{bal < 0}(account) |><| depositor)
C. Pi_{c-name}(sigma_{b-city = 'Agra' AND bal < 0}(branch |><| (b-city = 'Agra') account) |><| depositor)
D. Pi_{c-name}(sigma_{b-city = 'Agra'}(branch) |><| (sigma_{b-city = 'Agra'} account |><| depositor))
questions.to_find: The most efficient relational algebra expression equivalent to the given query

understand.plain: In relational algebra query optimization, we should push selections (sigma) as early (as close to the base relations) as possible to reduce the number of tuples before performing expensive join operations. This reduces intermediate result sizes. The query filters branches by city and accounts by balance, then joins with depositors.

keywords.explain: A query optimization technique where filter conditions (sigma) are applied before joins, reducing the size of intermediate results.
keywords.example: sigma_{b-city='Agra'}(branch) is applied first, before joining with account.

keywords.explain: Join operations are expensive; their cost depends on the sizes of the relations being joined. Reducing relation size before joining reduces cost.
keywords.example: If account has 1M tuples but only 10K have bal < 0, applying sigma_{bal<0} first reduces account to 10K before the join.

understand.visual_alt: Three relations: branch (small), account (large), depositor (large). The query joins all three after filtering by b-city='Agra' on branch and bal<0 on account. The most efficient plan pushes both selections before the joins.

given.aim: Identify the relational algebra expression that evaluates the query most efficiently.

terms.meaning: Branch is a small relation; account and depositor are much larger.
terms.example: branch might have 100 rows; account and depositor might have millions.
terms.connects: We should leverage the small branch size and filter large relations early.

terms.meaning: Selection filtering only branches in Agra city.
terms.example: From 100 branches, maybe only 5 are in Agra.
terms.connects: Applying this to branch first shrinks it further before joining.

terms.meaning: Selection filtering accounts with negative balance.
terms.example: From 1M accounts, maybe only 50K have bal < 0.
terms.connects: Applying this to account before joining drastically reduces join cost.

given.plan: Push both selection conditions (b-city='Agra' on branch and bal<0 on account) down to the base relations before performing joins. Option B does exactly this — it applies both selections early, then joins the filtered relations.

steps.title: Analyze each option for selection pushdown
steps.formula_raw: sigma_{cond}(R |><| S) = sigma_{cond}(R) |><| S  when cond involves only R's attributes
steps.apply:
Option A: applies sigma_{b-city='Agra'} inside branch join with account — but does NOT apply sigma_{bal<0} to account early. The bal<0 filter appears after joining branch with account, which is less efficient.
Option B: applies sigma_{b-city='Agra'} to branch AND sigma_{bal<0} to account separately before joining — both selections are pushed down optimally.
Option C: tries to apply b-city='Agra' to account which doesn't have that attribute directly — this is semantically questionable and inefficient.
Option D: applies sigma_{b-city='Agra'} twice (redundantly) and doesn't push bal<0 to account early.
steps.note: The key principle: push each selection to the smallest applicable relation before any joins.

steps.title: Select the most efficient option
steps.apply: Option B reduces both the branch relation (by city filter) and account relation (by bal filter) before joining. Since account and depositor are much bigger than branch, filtering account early with bal<0 gives the most significant reduction in join cost. The join order is: filtered_branch |><| filtered_account |><| depositor.
steps.note: Option B correctly pushes both conditions to their respective base relations.

solution.result: Option B is the most efficient. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Selection pushdown (predicate pushdown) in relational algebra: apply sigma as early as possible to reduce intermediate result sizes before expensive join operations.

questions.question:
Consider the following implications relating to functional and multivalued dependencies given below, which may or may not be correct:
i. if A -> B and A -> C then A ->-> BC
ii. if A ->-> B and A ->-> C then A -> BC
iii. if A -> B then A ->-> B
iv. if A ->-> BC and A -> B then A -> C

Exactly how many of the above implications are valid?
A. 0
B. 1
C. 2
D. 3
questions.to_find: How many of the four implications about functional and multivalued dependencies are valid

understand.plain: Functional dependency (FD) A->B means each A value determines exactly one B value. Multivalued dependency (MVD) A->>B means each A value is associated with a set of B values, independently of other attributes. We need to check which of the four implications between FDs and MVDs are logically valid.

keywords.explain: A->B means for every two tuples with the same A value, they have the same B value.
keywords.example: Student_ID -> Name means each student ID maps to exactly one name.

keywords.explain: A->>B means the set of B values associated with an A value is independent of other attributes in the relation.
keywords.example: Employee->>Skills means an employee's skills are independent of their projects.

keywords.explain: Every FD is also an MVD: if A->B then A->>B. This is because a single B value is trivially a set of one.
keywords.example: If SSN->Name, then SSN->>Name holds as well.

understand.visual_alt: A Venn-like containment: FDs are a subset of MVDs. Every functional dependency A->B implies A->>B. But MVDs do not necessarily imply FDs.

given.aim: Determine which of the four implications between FDs and MVDs are valid.

terms.meaning: Functional dependency: A functionally determines B.
terms.connects: FDs are a special case of MVDs.

terms.example: Course ->> Instructor (a course can have multiple instructors independently of textbooks).
terms.connects: MVDs generalize FDs; every FD is an MVD but not vice versa.

terms.meaning: Rules: (1) Every FD is an MVD. (2) Complementation: if A->>B in R(A,B,C) then A->>C. (3) Union: if A->>B and A->>C then A->>BC.
terms.example: From A->B we can derive A->>B by FD-to-MVD promotion.
terms.connects: Use these to verify/refute each implication.

given.plan: Evaluate each implication one by one using MVD axioms and known properties of FDs and MVDs.

steps.title: Check implication i: if A->B and A->C then A->>BC
steps.formula_raw: A->B => A->>B; A->C => A->>C; MVD union: A->>B and A->>C => A->>BC
steps.apply: From A->B, by FD-to-MVD rule: A->>B. From A->C: A->>C. By MVD union rule: A->>BC. So implication i is VALID.
steps.note: This follows from: every FD implies an MVD, and MVD union rule applies.

steps.title: Check implication ii: if A->>B and A->>C then A->BC
steps.formula_raw: MVD does not imply FD in general
steps.apply: An MVD A->>B does NOT imply A->B. For example, Course->>Instructor and Course->>Textbook are MVDs but Course does not functionally determine Instructor or Textbook (one course has many instructors). So implication ii is INVALID.
steps.note: MVDs are weaker than FDs; you cannot derive an FD from MVDs alone.

steps.title: Check implication iii: if A->B then A->>B
steps.formula_raw: Every FD is an MVD (promotion rule)
steps.apply: This is a standard axiom: every functional dependency A->B implies the multivalued dependency A->>B. Implication iii is VALID.
steps.note: This is one of the basic MVD inference rules derived from Armstrong's axioms extended to MVDs.

steps.title: Check implication iv: if A->>BC and A->B then A->C
steps.apply: Consider a counterexample: Let R(A, B, C) with A->>BC meaning A multidetermines the set {B,C}. Suppose A->B holds (A determines B). Does A->C follow? Not necessarily. Example: A=1 maps to (B=2,C=3) and (B=2,C=4). A->B holds (A always gives B=2). But A does NOT determine C (C can be 3 or 4). So A->C does not follow. Implication iv is INVALID.
steps.note: Having A->>BC means the pair (B,C) varies with A as a set; A->B just pins B but C can still vary.

steps.title: Count valid implications
steps.apply: Valid implications: i and iii. Invalid: ii and iv. Count = 2.

solution.result: Exactly 2 implications (i and iii) are valid. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Key rules: (1) Every FD A->B implies MVD A->>B. (2) MVD union rule: A->>B and A->>C implies A->>BC. (3) MVDs do not in general imply FDs.

questions.question:
Let r be a relation instance with schema R = (A, B, C, D). We define r1 = Pi_{A,B,C}(r) and r2 = Pi_{A,D}(r). Let s = r1 |><| r2 where |><| denotes natural join. Given that the decomposition of r into r1 and r2 is lossy, which one of the following is TRUE?
A. s subset-of r
B. r subset-of s
C. r = s
D. r intersect s = empty set
questions.to_find: The relationship between r and s = r1 |><| r2 when the decomposition is lossy

understand.plain: When we decompose a relation r into r1 and r2 by projection, then reconstruct by natural join, we get s = r1 |><| r2. If the decomposition is lossless, s = r exactly. If it is lossy, the join produces spurious (extra) tuples that were not in the original r, so r is a proper subset of s.

keywords.explain: A decomposition where natural joining the projections recreates the original relation exactly (no spurious tuples).
keywords.example: If R = (A,B,C) and A->B holds, decomposing into (A,B) and (A,C) is lossless.

keywords.explain: A decomposition where natural joining the projections produces extra (spurious) tuples not in the original relation.
keywords.example: If R = (A,B,C) with no FDs, decomposing into (A,B) and (B,C) may yield spurious tuples when joined.

keywords.explain: Tuples in s = r1 |><| r2 that were not in the original r; they arise due to incorrect join combinations.
keywords.example: If (a1,b1,c1) and (a2,b1,c2) are in r, a spurious tuple (a1,b1,c2) might appear in r1 |><| r2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="420" height="200" viewBox="0 0 420 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="60" width="180" height="110" rx="8" class="hi"/><text x="100" y="88" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">s = r1 |>| r2</text><text x="100" y="110" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">(larger set)</text><rect x="50" y="110" width="100" height="50" rx="6" class="ok"/><text x="100" y="142" text-anchor="middle" class="tag" fill="white">r (original)</text><text x="230" y="100" class="sub ink">Lossy: s has</text><text x="230" y="120" class="sub ink">spurious tuples</text><text x="230" y="140" class="sub ink">r subset-of s</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A large set s = r1 natural-join r2 contains the original r as a subset. Spurious tuples are the extra elements in s not in r.

given.aim: Determine the set relationship between r and s = r1 |><| r2 when decomposition is lossy.

terms.meaning: Projection of r onto attributes A, B, C.
terms.connects: r1 retains part of the original information.

terms.meaning: Projection of r onto attributes A, D.
terms.connects: r2 retains A and D values; joins with r1 on attribute A.

terms.meaning: The join r1 |><| r2 produces more tuples than the original r (spurious tuples appear).
terms.example: r1 = {(1,2,3),(1,2,5)} joined with r2 = {(1,4),(1,6)} on A gives 4 tuples, but original r had only 2.
terms.connects: Every original tuple is recoverable (r subset-of s) but s has extras.

given.plan: Use the fundamental property of projections and natural joins: every tuple in r can be recovered in s = r1 |><| r2, because projecting r onto (A,B,C) and (A,D) then joining on A will always reproduce the original tuples. But if lossy, extra spurious tuples also appear, making r a proper subset of s.

steps.title: Show r subset-of s always holds
steps.formula_raw: For any t in r: Pi_{A,B,C}(t) in r1 and Pi_{A,D}(t) in r2
steps.apply: Take any tuple t = (a, b, c, d) in r. Then (a,b,c) is in r1 and (a,d) is in r2. When we compute r1 |><| r2, these two tuples join on A=a to produce (a,b,c,d) = t. So t is in s. This proves r subset-of s always (regardless of lossless or lossy).
steps.note: This direction (r subset-of s) always holds for any decomposition into projections.

steps.title: Determine what 'lossy' means for the direction
steps.formula_raw: Lossy: s != r, i.e., s has tuples not in r
steps.apply: If the decomposition is lossless, s = r exactly. If lossy, s contains extra spurious tuples, so r is a proper subset of s (r subset-of s and r != s). This rules out options A (s subset-of r), C (r = s), and D (empty intersection).
steps.note: Option A would require s subset-of r, meaning no spurious tuples appear in s, which contradicts the lossy assumption. Option D is impossible since r and s always share all of r's tuples.

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.formula_raw: r subset-of s (proper subset when lossy)
steps.apply: For a lossy decomposition: r subset-of s (all original tuples are in s) but s also contains spurious tuples not in r, so s is strictly larger than r. The correct relationship is r subset-of s.
steps.note: This is the fundamental theorem of decomposition: Pi_{A,B,C}(r) |><| Pi_{A,D}(r) always contains r as a subset.

questions.formula_note: Fundamental decomposition theorem: for any relation r and attributes X, Y with X union Y = R, we have r subset-of Pi_X(r) |><| Pi_Y(r); equality holds iff the decomposition is lossless.
